title: Cash-Back Mortgages — When the Lender Pays You at Funding
metaTitle: Cash-Back Mortgage in Canada — When It Makes Sense
metaDescription: Cash-back mortgages return 1–7% of your mortgage amount as cash at funding. Useful for closing costs and renovations — when the higher rate is worth it.
intro: A cash-back mortgage gives you a lump sum at funding — typically 1–7% of the mortgage amount — in exchange for a higher rate over the term. Used carefully, it can fund closing costs, basic renovations or moving expenses. Used carelessly, it costs more than it gives.

body.heading: How the math works
paragraphs: Suppose you take a $700,000 mortgage with 5% cash back ($35,000) at a rate that's 0.50% higher than market. Over a 5-year term, that 0.50% premium costs you roughly $17,500 in extra interest. Net benefit at the 5-year mark: $17,500. Worth it if you can put the cash to better use elsewhere; not worth it if you'd otherwise just pay the lower rate.

Two real catches: most cash-back mortgages have a clawback clause. If you break or pay off the mortgage early, the lender claws back a pro-rated portion of the cash advance. So plan to stay full term.

whoItsFor: Need closing-cost or moving-cost cash and don't want to drain your down payment
Plan to stay in the mortgage for the full term
Will use the cash productively (renovations, debt paydown)

faqs.question: Can I use the cash for the down payment?
faqs.answer: No — cash-back funds advance at closing, after the mortgage is registered. Down payment must come from your own savings or other accepted sources.

faqs.question: Are cash-back mortgages a good deal?
faqs.answer: Sometimes. Run the math — extra interest cost over the term vs. the cash benefit. If you'd just save the money, the lower-rate non-cash-back mortgage almost always wins.
